author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

title: Homily XLVII on Acts xxi. 39, 40.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Paul said, I am a man which am a Jew of Tarsus, a city in Cilicia, a citizen of no mean city: and I beseech thee, suffer me to speak unto the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he had given him license, Paul stood on the stairs, and beckoned with the hand unto the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when there was made a great silence, he spake unto them in the Hebrew tongue, saying.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how, when he discourses to those that are without, he does not decline availing himself of the aids afforded by the laws.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he awes the tribune by the name of his city.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again, elsewhere he said, “Openly, uncondemned, Romans as we are, they have cast us into prison.” (ch. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For since the tribune said, “Art thou that Egyptian?” he immediately drew him off from that surmise: then, that he may not be thought to deny his nation, he says at once, “I am a Jew:” he means his religion. (b) What then? he did not deny (that he was a Christian): God forbid: for he was both a Jew and a Christian, observing what things he ought: since indeed he, most of all men, did obey the law: (a) as in fact he elsewhere calls himself, “Under the law to Christ.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is this, I pray? (c) The man that believes in Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when discoursing with Peter, he says: “We, Jews by nature.—But I beseech thee, suffer me to speak unto the people.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this is a proof, that he does not speak lies, seeing he takes all as his witnesses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe again how mildly he speaks.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This again is a very strong argument that he is chargeable with no crime, his being so ready to make his defence, and his wishing to come to discourse with the people of the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See a man well-prepared (τεταγμένον ἄνδρα)!—Mark the providential ordering of the thing: unless the tribune had come, unless he had bound him, he would not have desired to speak for his defence, he would not have obtained the silence he did.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Standing on the stairs.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then there was the additional facility afforded by the locality, that he should have a high place to harangue them from—in chains too!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What spectacle could be equal to this, to see Paul, bound with two chains, and haranguing the people!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (To see him,) how he was not a whit perturbed, not a whit confused; how, seeing as he did so great a multitude all hostility against him, the ruler standing by, he first of all made them desist from their anger: then, how prudently (he does this).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just what he does in his Epistle to the Hebrews, the same he does here: first he attracts them by the sound of their common mother tongue: then by his mildness itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He spake unto them,” it says, “in the Hebrew tongue, saying, Men, brethren, and fathers, hear ye my defence which I make now unto you.” (ch. xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark his address, at once so free from all flattery, and so expressive of meekness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he says not, “Masters,” nor “Lords,” but, “Brethren,” just the word they most liked: “I am no alien from. you,” he says, nor “against you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Men,” he says, “brethren, and fathers:” this, a term of honor, that of kindred.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Hear ye,” says he, “my”—he says not, “teaching,” nor “harangue,” but, “my defence which I now make unto you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He puts himself in the posture of a suppliant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they heard that he spake in the Hebrew tongue to them, they kept the more silence.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe how the using the same tongue subdued them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, they had a sort of awe for that language.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe also how he prepares the way for his discourse, beginning thus: “I am verily a man which am a Jew, born in Tarsus, a city in Cilicia, yet brought up in this city at the feet of Gamaliel, and taught according to the perfect manner of the law of the fathers, and was zealous toward God, as ye all are this day.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I am a man,” he says, “which am a Jew:” which thing they liked most of all to hear; “born in Tarsus, a city of Cilicia.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That they may not again think him to be of another nation, he adds his religion: “but brought up in this city.” (p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows how great was his zeal for the worship, inasmuch as having left his native city, which was so great and so remote too, he chose to be brought up here for the Law’s sake.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how from the beginning he attached himself to the law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this he says, not only to defend himself to them, but to show that not by human intent was he led to the preaching of the Gospel, but by a Divine power: else, having been so educated, he would not have suddenly changed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if indeed he had been one of the common order of men, it might have been reasonable to suspect this: but if he was of the number of those who were most of all bound by the law, it was not likely that he should change lightly, and without strong necessity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But perhaps some one may say: “To have been brought up here proves nothing: for what if thou camest here for the purpose of trading, or for some other cause?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore he says, “at the feet of Gamaliel:” and not simply, “by Gamaliel,” but “at his feet,” showing his perseverance, his assiduity, his zeal for the hearing, and his great reverence for the man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Taught according to the perfect manner of the law of the fathers.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not simply, “the law,” but “the law of the fathers;” showing that he was such from the beginning, and not merely one that knew the Law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All this seems indeed to be spoken on their side, but in fact it told against them, since he, knowing the law, forsook it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yes: but what if thou didst indeed know the law accurately, but dost not vindicate it, no, nor love it?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Being a zealot,” he adds: not simply (one that knew it).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, since it was a high encomium he had passed upon himself, he makes it theirs as well as his, adding, “As ye all are this day.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he shows that they act not from any human object, but from zeal for God; gratifying them, and preoccupying their minds, and getting a hold upon them in a way that did no harm.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he brings forward proofs also, saying, “and I persecuted this way unto the death, binding and delivering into prisons both men and women.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As also the high priest doth bear me witness, and all the estate of the elders” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4, 5): “How does this appear.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As witnesses he brings forward the high-priest himself and the elders.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He says indeed, “Being a zealot, as ye” (Hom. xix. p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 123): but he shows by his actions, that he went beyond them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For I did not wait for an opportunity of seizing them: I both stirred up the priests, and undertook journeys: I did not confine my attacks, as ye did, to men, I extended them to women also: “both binding, and casting into prisons both men and women.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This testimony is incontrovertible; the (unbelief) of the Jews (is left) without excuse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how many witnesses he brings forward, the elders, the high-priest, and those in the city.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe his defence, how it is not of cowardly fear (for himself, that he pleads), no, but for teaching and indoctrination.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For had not the hearers been stones, they would have felt the force of what he was saying.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For up to this point he had themselves as witnesses: the rest, however, was without witnesses: “From whom also I received letters unto the brethren, and went to Damascus, to bring them which were there bound unto Jerusalem, for to be punished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it came to pass, that, as I made my journey, and was come nigh unto Damascus about noon, suddenly there shone from heaven a great light round about me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And I fell unto the ground, and heard a voice saying unto me, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou Me?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And I answered, Who are Thou, Lord?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said unto me, I am Jesus of Nazareth, Whom thou persecutest.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, these very things ought to have been held worthy of credit, from those that went before: otherwise he would not have undergone such a revolution.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How if he is only making a fine story of it, say you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Answer me, Why did he suddenly fling away all this zeal?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because he looked for honor?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet he got just the contrary.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But an easy life, perhaps?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, nor that either.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well but something else?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why it is not in the power of thought to invent any other object.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So then, leaving it to themselves to draw the inference, he narrates the facts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As I came nigh,” he says, “unto Damascus, about noonday.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how great was the excess of the light.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What if he is only making a fine story, say you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Those who were with him are witnesses, who led him by the hand, who saw the light.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they that were with me saw indeed the light, and were afraid; but they heard not the voice of Him that spake to me.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in another place he says, “Hearing the voice, but seeing no man.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not at variance: no, there were two voices, that of Paul and the Lord’s voice: in that place, the writer means Paul’s voice (Hom. xix. p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 124, note 2); as in fact (Paul) here adds, “The voice of Him that spake unto me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seeing no man:” he does not say, that they did not see the light: but, “no man,” that is, “none speaking.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And good reason that it should be so, since it behooved him alone to have that voice vouchsafed unto him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if indeed they also had heard it, (the miracle) would not have been so great.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since persons of grosser minds are persuaded more by sight, those saw the light, and were afraid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, neither did the light take so much effect on them, as it did on him: for it even blinded his eyes: by that which befel him, (God) gave them also an opportunity of recovering their sight, if they had the mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me at least, that their not believing was providentially ordered, that they might be unexceptionable witnesses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he said unto me” it says, “I am Jesus of Nazareth, Whom thou persecutest.” (comp. ch. ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well is the name of the city (Nazareth) also added, that they might recognize (the Person): moreover, the Apostles also spoke thus. (ch. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Himself bore witness, that they were persecuting Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they that were with me saw indeed the light, and were afraid, but they heard not the voice of Him that spake to me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And I said, What shall I do, Lord?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the Lord said unto me, Arise, and go into Damascus; and there it shall be told thee of all things which are appointed for thee to do.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when I could not see for the glory of that light, being led by the hand of them that were with me, I came into Damascus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And one Ananias, a devout man according to the law, having a good report of all the Jews which dwelt there, came unto me, and stood, and said unto me, Brother Saul, receive thy sight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the same hour I looked up upon him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Enter into the city,” it says, “and there it shall be spoken to thee of all that is appointed for thee to do.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo! again another witness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see how unexceptionable he makes him also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And one Ananias,” he says, “a devout man according to the law,”—so far is it from being anything alien!—“having a good report of all the Jews that dwelt” (there).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And I in the same hour received sight.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then follows the testimony borne by the facts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how it is interwoven, of persons and facts; and the persons, both of their own and of aliens: the priests, the elders, and his fellow-travellers: the facts, what he did and what was done to him: and facts bear witness to facts, not persons only.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Ananias, an alien; then the fact itself, the recovery of sight; then a great prophecy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he said, The God of our fathers hath chosen thee, that thou shouldest know His will, and see That Just One.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is well said, “Of the fathers,” to show that they were not Jews, but aliens from the law, and that it was not from zeal (for the law) that they were acting.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “That thou shouldest know His will.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then His will is this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how in the form of narrative it is teaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And see That Just One, and hear the voice of His mouth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For thou shalt be His witness unto all men of what thou hast seen and heard.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see,” he says, “that Just One.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the present he says no more than this: if He is Just, they are guilty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And hear the voice of His mouth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how high he raises the fact!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For thou shalt be His witness—for this, because thou wilt not betray the sight and hearing (i.e. “prove false to”)—“both of what thou hast seen, and of what thou hast heard:” by means of both the senses he claims his faith, fulness—“to all men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And now why tarriest thou? arise, and be baptized, and wash away thy sins, calling on His name.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here it is a great thing he has uttered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he said not, “Be baptized in His name:” but, “calling on the name of Christ.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows that He is God: since it is not lawful to “call upon” any other, save God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he shows also, that he himself was not compelled: for, “I said,” says he, “What must I do?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing is (left) without witness: no; he brings forward the witness of a whole city, seeing they had beheld him led by the hand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But see the prophecy fulfilled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To all men,” it is said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he did become a witness to Him, and a witness as it ought to be; by what he suffered, by what he did, and by what he said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such witnesses ought we also to be, and not to betray the things we have been entrusted withal: I speak not only of doctrines, but also of the manner of life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For observe: because he had seen, because he had heard, he bears witness to all men, and nothing hindered him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We too bear witness (Mod. text “have heard”) that there is a Resurrection and numberless good things: we are bound to bear witness of this to all men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yes, and we do bear witness,” you will say, “and do believe.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How; when ye act the contrary?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say now: if any one should call himself a Christian, and then having apostatized should hold with the Jews, would this testimony suffice?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no means: for men would desire the testimony which is borne by the actions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just so, if we say that there is a Resurrection and numberless good things, and then despise those things and prefer the things here, who will believe us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not what we say, but what we do, is what all men look to.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thou shalt be a witness,” it says, “unto all men:” not only to the friendly, but also to the unbelievers: for this is what witnesses are for; not to persuade those who know, but those who know not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us be trustworthy witnesses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But how shall we be trustworthy?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By the life we lead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jews assaulted him: our passions assault us, bidding us abjure our testimony.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us not obey them: we are witnesses from God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Christ) is judged that He is not God: He has sent us to bear witness to Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us bear witness and persuade those who have to decide the point: if we do not bear witness, we have to answer for their error also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if in a court of justice, where worldly matters come in question, nobody would receive a witness full of numberless vices, much less here, where such (and so great) are the matters to be considered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We say, that we have heard Christ, and that we believe the things which He has promised: Show it, say they, by your works: for your life bears witness of the contrary—that ye do not believe.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, shall we look at the money-getting people, the rapacious, the covetous? the people that mourn and wail, that build and busy themselves in all sorts of things, as though they were never to die?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye do not believe that ye shall die, a thing so plain and evident: and how shall we believe you when ye bear witness?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there are, there are many men, whose state of mind is just as if they were not to die.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when in a lengthened old age they set about building and planting, when will they take death into their calculations?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It will be no small punishment to us that we were called to bear witness, but were not able to bear witness of the things that we have seen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We have seen Angels with our eyes, yea, more clearly than those who have (visibly) beheld them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We shall be (Mod. text “Then let us be”) witnesses to Christ: for not those only are “martyrs,” (or witnesses, whom we so call), but ourselves also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why they are called martyrs, because when bidden to abjure (the faith), they endure all things, that they may speak the truth: and we, when we are bidden by our passions to abjure, let us not be overcome.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Gold saith: Say that Christ is not Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then listen not to it as to God, but despise its biddings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The evil lusts “profess that they know God, but in works they deny Him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is not to witness, but the contrary.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And indeed that others should deny (Him) is nothing wonderful: but that we who have been called to bear witness should deny Him, is a grievous and a heinous thing: this of all things does the greatest hurt to our cause.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “It shall be to (your)selves for a testimony.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 13), He saith: but (this is) when we ourselves stand to it firmly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we would all bear witness to Christ, we should quickly persuade the greater number of the heathen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a great thing, my beloved, the life (one leads).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let a man be savage as a beast, let him openly condemn thee on account of thy doctrine, yet he secretly approves, yet he will praise, yet he will admire.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, whence can an excellent life proceed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From no source, except from a Divine Power working in us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What if there be heathen also of such a character?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If anywhere any of them be such, it is partly from nature, partly from vainglory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wilt thou learn what a brilliancy there is in a good life, what a force of persuasion it has?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many of the heretics have thus prevailed, and while their doctrines are corrupt, yet the greater part of men out of reverence for their (virtuous) life did not go on to examine their doctrine: and many even condemning them on account of their doctrine, reverence them on account of their life: not rightly indeed, but still so it is, that they do thus feel (towards them).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This has brought slanders on the awful articles of our creed, this has turned everything upside down, that no one takes any account of good living: this is a mischief to the faith.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We say that Christ is God; numberless other arguments we bring forward, and this one among the rest, that He has persuaded all men to live rightly: but this is the case with few.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The badness of the life is a mischief to the doctrine of the Resurrection, to that of the immortality of the soul, to that of the Judgment: many other (false doctrines) too it draws on with itself, fate, necessity, denial of a Providence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the soul being immersed in numberless vices, by way of consolations to itself tries to devise these, that it may not be pained in having to reflect that there is a Judgment, and that virtue and vice lie in our own power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Such a) life works numberless evils, it makes men beasts, and more irrational than beasts: for what things are in each several nature of the beasts, these it has often collected together in one man, and turned everything upside down.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why the devil has brought in the doctrine of Fate: this is why he has said that the world is without a Providence (Hom. ii. p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15): this is why he advances his hypothesis of good natures, and evil natures, and his hypothesis of evil (uncreated and) without beginning, and material (in its essence): and, in short, all the rest of it, that he may ruin our life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is not possible for a man who is of such a life either to recover himself from corrupt doctrines, or to remain in a sound faith: but of inevitable necessity he must receive all this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For I do not think, for my part, that of those who do not live aright, there could be easily found any who do not hold numberless satanical devices—as, that there is a nativity (or birth-fate) (γένεσις), that things happen at random, that all is hap-hazard and chance-medley.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore I beseech you let us have a care for good living, that we may not receive evil doctrines.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Cain received for punishment that he should be (ever) groaning and trembling.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such are the wicked, and being conscious within themselves of numberless bad things, often they start out of their sleep, their thoughts are full of tumult, their eyes full of perturbation; everything is fraught for them with misgivings, everything alarms them, their soul is replete with grievous expectation and cowardly apprehension, contracted with impotent fear and trembling.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing can be more effeminate than such a soul, nothing more inane.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Like madmen, it has no self-possession.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it were well for it that in the enjoyment of calm and quiet it were enabled to take knowledge of its proper nobility.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when all things terrify and throw it into perturbation, dreams, and words, and gestures, and forebodings, indiscriminately, when will it be able to look into itself, being thus troubled and amazed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us therefore do away with its fear, let us break asunder its bonds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For were there no other punishment, what punishment could exceed this—to be living always in fear, never to have confidence, never to be at ease?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore knowing these things assuredly, let us keep ourselves in a state of calm and be careful to practise virtue, that maintaining both sound doctrines and an upright life, we may without offence pass through this life present, and be enabled to attain unto the good things which God hath promised to them that love Him, through the grace and mercy of His only-begotten Son, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
